payload.summary: Evaluated three amendments against the codebase. Amendment fr-4-0dab1450 identifies a real observability gap where .ok() silently swallows all errors including corruption and permission failures. Amendment fr-4-e29e2ea2 suggests helpful documentation for the deny_unknown_fields enforcement strategy. Amendment fr-4-e57cd3d1 mischaracterizes an intentional design decision as a bug — legacy rows cannot be safely backfilled because the current snapshot hash may not match the plan they originally ran under.

votes.rationale: The .ok() call in snapshot_plan_hash() treats NotFound (expected: no snapshot yet) identically to PermissionDenied, CorruptRecord, and other I/O errors (unexpected). The codebase already demonstrates the pattern of distinguishing NotFound from other errors elsewhere (e.g., read_task_runs_from_path at lines 2579-2583 uses match on e.kind()). A corrupt snapshot silently preventing plan_hash auto-population is a real operational hazard with no signal to operators. Adding a warning log for non-NotFound errors is low-risk, high-value, and consistent with the existing error handling philosophy in the codebase.

The deny_unknown_fields + round-trip test pair is a reasonable enforcement mechanism, but its design intent is not self-evident from the serde annotation alone. While the existing test doc-comments explain what the tests verify ('If a field is added to the render payload but not to the parsing struct, the round-trip will lose it'), they don't explain the broader two-layer strategy: deny_unknown_fields provides runtime parse failure (triggering journal repair fallback), and the round-trip tests surface field mismatches at test time. A brief doc-comment on the structs or a note clarifying this is the chosen enforcement mechanism for the acceptance criterion would help future developers understand that these annotations and tests are load-bearing, not incidental.

The amendment mischaracterizes an intentional, well-documented design decision as a bug. The code comments at lines 2916-2920 and 3246-3250 explicitly explain: 'persist_plan() can advance snapshot.plan_hash after the original attempt started, so stamping the current snapshot hash would mislabel a v1 attempt as v2.' Legacy rows with plan_hash: null represent attempts where we genuinely do not know which plan version was executing. Backfilling them with the current snapshot hash would create false lineage (claiming a row ran under plan v2 when it may have run under v1 or no plan at all). Null means 'unknown' — that is correct. A wrong hash pretending to be known would be worse than missing data. The tests at lines 5122-5308 properly codify this invariant and protect against the mislabeling hazard. They are not 'locking in lossiness' — they are preventing data corruption.
